questions.prompt: An 71-year-old woman walks into your family practice office, complaining of a headache that started recently and scalp tenderness when she combs her hair. On fundoscopy you notice a swollen optic disc and flame hemorrhages. What is the most likely immediate treatment?

options.label: a
options.text: Refer to ophthalmology intra-ocular injection of anti-VEGF antibodies.

options.label: b
options.text: Prescribe NSAIDs, provide reassurance and advise patient for better sleep hygiene

options.label: d
options.text: Refer to ophthalmology for laser therapy

options.label: e
options.text: Refer to optometry for a full eye exam

answer.explanation: High clinical suspicion of giant cell ateritis, immediate high dose steroid helps prevent vision loss.

questions.prompt: A 2-year old boy is brought into your family practice clinic for the well baby visit. You noticed the following image when checking his eyes with the ophthalmoscope. What is the most appropriate next step? i. Make an urgent referral to ophthalmology ii. Start topical steroid eyedrops immediately iii. Ask parents for previous close-up shots of the eyes for comparison iv. Refer to optometry for a more extensive eye exam v. Provide parents with reassurance that this is a self limiting condition and their son will grow out of it vi. Try ophthalmoscope from an different angle and reassess

answer.explanation: The photo depicts leukocoria in the right eye and it’s critical to rule out retinoblastoma, which warrants an urgent referral to ophthalmology. Topical steroids are better to be reserved for opthamologists to prescribe; past photos are helpful to get a sense of the disease progression; this is not physiological leukocoria, which typically seen when light source is 15° off axis.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statement is TRUE for presbycusis?

options.label: a
options.text: It is the most common conductive hearing loss in elderly

options.label: b
options.text: The loss is most common at higher frequency

options.label: d
options.text: Weber test will lateralize to the affected ear

answer.explanation: Presbycusis is a form of sensorineural hearing loss and typically affects both ears. It’s progressive and irreversible. Conductive hearing losses are typically lateralized to the affected ear.

questions.prompt: A 39-year old male complains of hearing loss. He is a construction worker and is exposed to loud noises daily. You suspect that he may have noise-induced hearing loss. Which of the following findings is the most consistent with this diagnosis?

options.label: a
options.text: An air-bone gap on audiometric testing

options.label: b
options.text: Bone conduction greater than air conduction bilaterally when performing a Rinne test

options.label: c
options.text: A characteristic 4000 Hz notch on an audiogram

options.label: d
options.text: Symptoms of vertigo and aural fullness

answer.explanation: Noise-induced hearing loss is sensineural and both air-bone gap on audiometry and bone > air on Rinne test suggest conductive hearing loss. Hearing loss plus vertigo symptoms would suggest Meniere’s Disease.

questions.prompt: A 36-year-old male presents to your family practice with a concern of dizziness. He says the episodes last for about 30 seconds, are typically triggered when he “moves too fast,” and when they occur it feels like the room is “spinning around him.” He denies any changes in hearing. He does not take any medications and his past medical history is unremarkable. Given the most likely diagnosis, what treatment would you suggest?

options.label: a
options.text: Reassurance, vestibular rehabilitation exercises, no pharmacologic treatment

options.label: b
options.text: Reassurance, rest, and symptomatic treatment (e.g. gravol)

options.label: c
options.text: Smoking cessation, reduced-salt diet, diuretics, anti-nausea drugs

options.label: d
options.text: Reassurance, NSAIDs, beta-blockers, calcium-channel blockers, tranquilizers, 5-HT agonists

answer.explanation: The stem suggests BPPV and it can be treated with particle reposition maneuvers, which are considered as a type of vestibular rehabilitation exercise. B describes symptomatic treatment for vestibular neuronitis. C describes treatment options for Meniere’s Diseases. D describes treamnt for migrainous vertigo.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a cause of death?

answer.explanation: Septic shock & cardiac arrhythmia are MECHANISMS of death, and suicide is a MANNER of death.

questions.prompt: A 25-year-old patient presents to your office with right-sided painless vision loss and a left RAPD. Upon examination, you find that their optic disc appears to be swollen. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: a
options.text: Reassure the patient it will resolve on its own

options.label: b
options.text: Consult with neuro-ophthalmology for management of optic neuritis

options.label: c
options.text: Consult with neurology to investigate underlying Multiple Sclerosis

answer.explanation: Optic neuritis is one of the symptoms of MS. For a patient of this age, a referral to neurology to rule out MS is indicated

questions.prompt: A 55-year-old patient presents to your office to follow up on an injury after a recent car accident. The patient tells you that they’ve been having trouble driving at night often seeing glares and haloes around lights. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answer.explanation: These symptoms are consistent with cataracts

questions.prompt: You are on your ER rotation and a 20-year-old patient presents with significant eye discharge that started in the last 10 hours. On history, the patient tells you she had sexual intercourse 2 days ago. She did not use any protection methods. What is the most likely diagnosis?

answer.explanation: Given the patient’s history and symptoms, this is consistent with a gonococcal conjunctivitis

questions.prompt: A patient presents to your office with red eye, worsening vision, ocular pain, and photophobia. On exam you find that their cornea looks hazy. The patient tells you that they recently replaced their glasses with contact lenses. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: a
options.text: Refer the patient to ophthalmology immediately

options.label: b
options.text: Advise the patient to continue wearing their lenses

options.label: c
options.text: Refer the patient to an optometrist

options.label: d
options.text: Start the patient on steroid medication

answer.explanation: These symptoms are consistent with contact lens associated keratitis. A referral to ophthalmology is indicated since this can lead to permanent vision loss

questions.prompt: A patient presents to your office with hearing loss that started 2 days ago. The patient also complains of fullness in their ears and feeling like the world around them is “spinning”. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: a
options.text: Order an MRI to rule out acoustic neuroma

options.label: b
options.text: Refer the patient to ENT urgently

answer.explanation: This is a case of sudden SNHL which is an otologic emergency requiring referral to ENT and an MRI to rule out acoustic neuroma

questions.prompt: Which of the following is associated with ototoxicity? Pick all that apply.

answer.explanation: All of these agents are ototoxic

questions.prompt: Which of the following is characteristic of benign positional paroxysmal vertigo (BPPV)? Pick all that apply
What is the best course of management for a patient presenting with the symptoms in Question 13?

answer.explanation: Particle repositioning is the first course of management for patients with BPPV

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a red flag for headaches? Pick all that apply

options.label: c
options.text: Associated with systemic symptoms (e.g. fever, chills)

answer.explanation: Headaches that are worse when supine rather than standing up are a red flag due to increased ICP. Acute onset rather than insidious onset headaches are also red flags

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a migraine aura? Pick all that apply
In the event of patient death, which of the following is an indication to contact the coroner?

answer.explanation: A patient dying of cancer is not in and of itself an indication to contact the coroner

questions.prompt: Which of the following is true about MAID?

options.label: a
options.text: Patients under 18 years old are eligible for MAID under the present law in Canada

options.label: b
options.text: A patient needs to give consent to MAID once during the process

options.label: c
options.text: Patient eligibility needs to be certified by 2 independent physicians or nurse practitioners

options.label: d
options.text: MAID must occur in a hospital

questions.prompt: Which of the following is associated with a white light reflex (leukocoria) in a child?
Which of the following is an indication for referral to a pediatric ophthalmologist? Pick all that apply

answer.explanation: A referral to an ophthalmologist is indicated if occasional deviation lasts beyond 3-4 months
